title: Negative outcomes of a vegan/vegetarian diet

chunks.title: Dietary Comparison of Children
chunks.summary: The segment discusses a study comparing dietary impacts on children aged 5 to 10. It highlights that vegan diets were associated with a healthier cardiovascular profile but also raised concerns about nutritional deficiencies, lower bone density, and height compared to omnivore diets.
chunks.transcript: which is an interesting thing they put that in there of five to 10 year old children consuming vegetarian vegan and omnivore diets they say vegan diets are associated with a healthier cardiovascular risk profile this is such a catch-all wastebasket thing these days it just means they had lower cholesterol which i would argue is a very bad thing they weren't able to substantiate any sort of proper hormonal levels but they had an increased risk of nutritional deficiencies and uh lower bone mineral density and height so oh no but it's okay they have lower ldl that's a good thing we all know that lower ldl is good um interestingly we should know if

chunks.title: Critique of Study Methodology
chunks.summary: The speaker critiques the methodology of the discussed study, highlighting that many vegan participants did not supplement with essential nutrients like B12 and vitamin D. This raises questions about the healthiness of the vegan population studied and whether the results are representative.
chunks.transcript: doctors they they debunked this study because a lot of the the vegans were not supplementing with b12 i think it was one-third of them or something like that um and not with vitamin d either and so they said that this is not a healthy vegan population it's an unhealthy vegan population and that is why the results are skewed in the way that they are because they weren't supplementing properly and and and but that just you know my argument that was well you know if if if we're advising human beings on diet shouldn't we take into account you know human behavior like forgetfulness or just laziness and these things that are inherent in human beings and and account for those things when recommending a diet to humans um like you know if we need so many supplements like it's it's it's like someone is going to forget to give the right dosage the right type of b12 and they're going to give methylcobalamin instead of cyanide element and make all kinds of mistakes like there's a lot of mistakes that can go wrong and so even though a lot of them forgot to get the right supplementation the it still showcases that um it's not optimal um and it's it's it's problematic and dangerous as well um so yeah i would say clearly not optimal vegans had lower fat indices in all

chunks.title: Nutritional Deficiencies in Vegans
chunks.summary: This segment focuses on various nutritional deficiencies found in vegans, including lower levels of protein, iron, and essential vitamins. The speaker emphasizes that the disadvantages in health outcomes heavily outweigh the perceived benefits of lower LDL cholesterol.
chunks.transcript: um and lower hdl um they had lower hscrp which is interesting but they had lower protein lower iron status lower serum b12 and lower 25 hydroxy vitamin d without supplementation and they had higher homocysteine and mean corpuscular volume homocysteine is an indication of b12 and or folate and or riboflavin deficiency with higher being worse and a higher mean corpuscular volume would indicate either folate or b12 deficiency so uh there's you know vitamin b12 deficiency iron deficiency anemia low ferritin which is indicative of low iron source and low hdl were more prevalent in vegans who also had the lowest prevalence of quote-unquote high ldl so it's just this doesn't look good for vegans

chunks.title: Evolutionary Diet Considerations
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the importance of evolutionary diet theory, questioning the viability of vegan diets based on ancestral human dietary practices. They mention the health status of indigenous populations as evidence for a more animal-based diet being crucial for growth and development.
chunks.transcript: our ancestors were really doing this is are we are we really so sure or we really believe that we can break from what our ancestors have been doing for so long you know i when i visited the hadza last year they didn't give their kids any supplements and they generally grow up pretty darn hail and hearty uh i think that it's clear that an evolutionarily appropriate species-appropriate diet for humans should be intuitive and easy and i love that kids really give us indication of this what do your kids prefer to eat

chunks.title: Choline as a Critical Nutrient
chunks.summary: This segment introduces choline as an essential nutrient that is often overlooked in discussions about vegan diets. The speaker argues that vegan diets are typically low in choline and highlights its critical roles in human health, especially for children.
chunks.transcript: the nutrients that i've never seen mentioned in any one of these vegan studies let's talk about choline uh this is an article called choline the underconsumed underappreciated essential nutrient this is in humans but it's incredible where's the choline on a vegan diet and any of these studies does cleveland clinic or webmd say you know actually your vegan kids are going to be low in choline vegetarian kids might not be if they're getting enough eggs and milk but a vegan child is definitely going to be low in choline well why do you need choline well to make every single cell membrane in your body neurons brain acetylcholine neurotransmitters it's pretty crazy stuff and here we you know see this um just talked about very clearly for um even adults and kids but there's

chunks.title: Choline Sources in Vegan Diets
chunks.summary: The speaker critiques the idea that vegan sources like soy and cauliflower provide adequate choline, arguing that these sources are not bioavailable compared to animal products. They stress that without sufficient choline intake, individuals, especially children, may face health risks.
chunks.transcript: choline in the vegan community there has been in the past when uh proponents of the paleo or ketogenic diet talk about it and talk about how vegan diets don't have choline and it's generally debunked by um them saying that soy and cauliflower have choline so those two foods soy and cauliflower yeah apparently those two foods are the are the answer to that question so you have to feed your kids tons of cauliflower every day and tons of choline every day soy soy soy based soy based choline which is clearly not as bioavailable as an animal-based form of choline uh okay so i think that we can i don't have that study pulled up right now but we can pause the podcast and pull it up but there's um it's it's pretty clear that vegans are low in choline even with all of the uh even with as much of the um i'm sure they're getting soy yeah yeah even with cauliflower and soy smoothies all day long they they don't seem to get enough choline in their diets so this one i think is the one that i wanted to pull up this one usual choli intakes are associated with egg and protein food consumption in the united states so this study um from july 2017 found that choline an essential nutrient for critical roles and several biological processes including neuronal development cell signaling nerve impulse transmission lipid transport and metabolism uh they found that protein food meat poultry and seafood consumption increased choline consumption and uh adults 19 plus who consumed eggs more likely to meet their gender and life stage adequate intakes as compared to non-consumers but basically if people didn't consume eggs or protein in this observational study um there were uh substantial deficiencies in choline which is a
